elements.sent-2411-004: We provide a large sample scaling analysis of the Zig-Zag process and its sub-sampling versions in settings of parametric Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-005: In the transient phase of the algorithm, we show that the Zig-Zag trajectories are well approximated by the solution to a system of ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-006: These ODEs possess a drift in the direction of decreasing KL-divergence between the assumed model and the true distribution and are explicitly characterized in the paper.

elements.sent-2411-008: Based on our results, we estimate that for large data sets of size $n$, using suitable control variates with sub-sampling in Zig-Zag, the algorithm costs $O(1)$ to obtain an essentially independent sample; a computational speed-up of $O(n)$ over the canonical version of Zig-Zag and other traditional MCMC methods.
elements.sent-2411-009: Piecewise deterministic Markov processes \citep[PDMPs,][]{davis1984piecewise are a class of non-reversible Markov processes which have recently emerged as alternatives to traditional MCMC methods based on the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Practically implementable PDMP algorithms such as the Zig-Zag sampler \citep{bierkens2019 , the Bouncy Particle Sampler \citep{bouchard2018bouncy and others \citep{corbella2022automatic,vasdekis2021speed,wu2020coordinate,bierkens2020boomerang,bertazzi2022adaptive,bertazzi2022approximations,pagani2024nuzz have provided highly encouraging results for Bayesian inference as well as in Physics \citep[for example]{krauth2021event .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: As a result, there has been a surge of interest in the Monte Carlo community to better understand the theoretical properties of PDMPs \citep[see e.g.][]{holderrieth2021cores,deligiannidis2021randomized,andrieu2021hypocoercivity,bierkens2017limit,bierkens2019ergodicity,bierkens2021large,bierkens2022high .}
elements.sent-2411-012: This body of work demonstrates powerful convergence properties with impressive scalability as a function of the dimension.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the most exciting property of PDMPs, at least for statistical applications is that of {\em principled sub-sampling \citep{bierkens2019 , whereby randomly chosen subsets of data can be used to estimate likelihood values within the algorithm without biasing its invariant distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The authors only cover sub-sampling for the Zig-Zag algorithm, although the unbiasedness property holds for a rather general class of PDMP MCMC algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-015: This unbiasing property is in sharp contrast to most MCMC methods \citep{welling2011bayesian,nemeth2021,bardenet2017markov , although see \cite{cornish2019scalable .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In fact, \cite{bierkens2019 gives experiments to show that even for data sets of large size $n$, subsets of size $1$ can be used to estimate the likelihood, producing a computational speed-up of $O(n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This leads to the property of {\em superefficiency whereby, after initialization, the entire running cost of the algorithm is dominated for large $n$ by the cost of evaluating the likelihood once.}

elements.sent-2411-027: Our contributions in this paper are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: For the Zig-Zag algorithm, we study the effect of sub-sampling on the dynamics of the underlying stochastic process as the data set size $n$ increases to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Theorem \ref{thm:transient , we give weak convergence results for the trajectories of the process to smooth solutions of a system of ODEs.}
elements.sent-2411-030: These ODEs can be explicitly characterized as presented in equation \eqref{eq:limiting_drift .}
elements.sent-2411-031: We also study the effect of sub-sampling on the algorithm mixing in the stationary phase.

elements.sent-2411-036: In Section \ref{sec:examples , we propose a mixed sub-sampling scheme which in addition to being exact, supersedes both ZZ-SS and ZZ-CV in performance.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, we outline how these methods can be generalised to more general classes of PDMPs.

elements.section-2411-002: Zig-Zag process for parametric Bayesian inference with i.i.d. observations
elements.sent-2411-068: In this section, we review how the Zig-Zag process can be designed to sample from a Bayesian posterior distribution in the setting where the data are assumed to be generated independently from a fixed probability model.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Designing Zig-Zag process for Bayesian inference
elements.sent-2411-070: The $d-$dimensional Zig-Zag process $(Z_t)_{t \ge 0 = (X_t, V_t)_{t \ge 0}$ is a continuous time Markov process that evolves on the augmented space $E = \mathbb{R ^d \times \mathcal{V}$ where $\mathcal{V = \{-1, +1\}^d$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We refer to $X_t$ and $V_t$ as {\it position and {\it velocity process respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The Zig-Zag process is completely characterized by a collection of continuous {\it switching rates $(\lambda_i)_{i=1 ^d$ where $\lambda_i: E \to [0, \infty)$ for all $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Given $(\lambda_i)_{i=1 ^d$, $(Z_t)_{t \ge 0}$ has right-continuous paths in $E$ such that, \begin{equation}X_t = X_0 + \int_0^t V_s\ ds, \quad t \ge 0,\end{equation} and the $i$-th coordinate of $(V_t)_{t \ge 0}$ jumps between $+1$ and $-1$ according to an inhomogeneous Poisson process of intensity $\lambda_i(Z_t)$ independently of other coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Given $n$ independent and identically distributed data points $\bs{y ^{(n) = (y_1, \dots, y_n)$, consider the classical Bayesian posterior density, $\pi^{(n)}$, given by \begin{equation}\pi^{(n)}(x) \propto \exp\left(\log \pi_0(x) + \sum_{j=1}^n \log f(Y_j; x)\right); \quad x \in R^d,\end{equation} for some probability density function $f$ and prior density $\pi_0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Define the potential function $U_n$ by, \begin{equation}\label{eq:potential} U_n(x) = -\log\pi^{(n)}(x) = -\log \pi_0(x) -\sum_{j=1}^n \log f(y_j; x) + C(\bs{y}^{(n)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: For $i = 1, \dots, d$, define the flip operator $F_i: \mathcal{V \to \mathcal{V}$, which flips the $i$-th component of $v$: for $v \in \mathcal{V}$, $(F_i(v))_i = -v_i$ and $(F_i(v))_j = v_j$ for $j \neq i$.}

elements.sent-2411-079: A necessary and sufficient condition for \eqref{eq:suff_rates to hold is that there exists a continuous $\gamma^n:E \to [0, \infty)^d$ such that $\gamma^n_i(x, v) = \gamma_i(x, F_i(v))$ for all $(x, v) \in E$, and \begin{equation}\label{eq:rates} \lambda^n_i(x, v) = (v_i \nabla_iU_n(x))_{+} + \gamma^n_i(x, v), \quad (x, v) \in E, \ i = 1, \dots, d.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-415: In this paper, we study the sub-sampling versions of the Zig-Zag algorithm in the settings of Bayesian inference with big data.
elements.sent-2411-416: Our objective in undertaking this study has been to comment on the algorithmic complexity of these algorithms as a function of data size $n$.
elements.sent-2411-417: We do so by analyzing the behaviour of the underlying Zig-Zag process when $n$ goes to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-418: Based on our results, we estimate that ZZ-CV has a total algorithmic complexity of $O(1)$ in stationarity while both canonical Zig-Zag and ZZ-SS have $O(n)$.
elements.sent-2411-419: This reveals that the Zig-Zag algorithm is no worse than other reversible MCMC algorithms such as the random walk Metropolis which also has a complexity of $O(n)$ in stationarity \citep{schmon2021optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-420: Moreover, the control variates can be utilized to gain an order of magnitude in total computational cost further.
elements.sent-2411-421: Our results also provide insights into the convergence time of the Zig-Zag samplers initiated out from stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-422: As expected, the canonical Zig-Zag converges to stationarity with optimal speed.
elements.sent-2411-423: The sub-sampling versions remain sub-optimal in general.
elements.sent-2411-424: However, while the corresponding drift for ZZ-SS goes to $0$ in stationarity, ZZ-CV remains positive achieving optimal speed for log-concave model densities.
elements.sent-2411-425: Based on the above observations, we infer strong support for the use of the Zig-Zag algorithm for sampling from Bayesian posteriors for big data.
elements.sent-2411-426: However, we also caution that the superefficient performance of ZZ-CV heavily relies on good reference points.
elements.sent-2411-427: Inappropriately chosen reference points can lead to undesirable behaviours as discussed in Sections \ref{sec:stationary and \ref{sec:examples .}}
elements.sent-2411-428: To mitigate the effect of bad reference points in the transient phase, we propose a mixed scheme whereby vanilla sub-sampling is done in the initial phases of the algorithm and the control variates are introduced later when the sampler is close to stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-429: We empirically show that such a scheme can achieve faster convergence than ZZ-SS and ZZ-CV.
elements.sent-2411-430: Nevertheless, this also calls for investigation into more sophisticated control variates and can be a direction of future research.
elements.sent-2411-431: The asymptotic behaviour of both ZZ-SS and ZZ-CV depends on the choice of the batch size $m$.
elements.sent-2411-432: Theoretically, large batch size $m$ implies faster convergence and better mixing.
elements.sent-2411-433: In practice, larger $m$ also means additional costs.
elements.sent-2411-434: We show that it is optimal to choose batch size $m = 1$ in terms of total algorithmic complexity.
elements.sent-2411-435: Secondly, we assume that the subsamples are drawn randomly without replacement.
elements.sent-2411-436: As a result, we can invoke the law of large numbers for $U$-statistics to obtain our limit results.
elements.sent-2411-437: There is no added generality in the results in allowing sampling with replacement.
elements.sent-2411-438: The switching rates then resemble $V-$statistics of order $m$ which can be expressed explicitly as a combination of $U$-statistics and the strong law of large numbers still hold \citep{lee2019u .}
elements.sent-2411-439: In practice, however, it is better to do sampling without replacement as it leads to a smaller variance during the implementation.

elements.sent-2411-750: Piecewise deterministic Markov processes (PDMPs, \cite{davis1984piecewise ) are a class of non-reversible Markov processes which have recently emerged as alternatives to traditional MCMC methods based on the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-751: As a result, there has been a surge of interest in the Monte Carlo community to better understand the theoretical properties of PDMPs \citep[see e.g.][]{holderrieth2021cores,deligiannidis2021randomized,andrieu2021hypocoercivity,bierkens2017limit,bierkens2019ergodicity,bierkens2021large,bierkens2022high,bierkens_scaling_2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-752: For largely good reasons, much of MCMC theory has focused on the behaviour of algorithms as a function of the dimension However, within applications in Bayesian Statistics, it is at least as important to consider how sampler properties vary with data.
elements.sent-2411-753: Theoretical studies to focus on this problem are relatively rare (though see \cite{altmeyerPolynomialTimeGuarantees2022,sahu_convergence_1999, ascolani_scalability_2024, ascolani_dimension-free_2024, zanella_multilevel_2021, yang_computational_2016 ).}
elements.sent-2411-754: The main reason for this is that studying the data effect is fraught with additional technical complications making rigorous results hard to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-755: To our knowledge this paper is the first to attempt to make progress on this problem for PDMPs.
elements.sent-2411-756: Furthermore, the emergence of {\em sub-sampling methodology as outlined in the next paragraph, makes analysis of algorithm complexity as function of data size $n$ of great practical importance.}
elements.sent-2411-757: From a practical MCMC perspective, the most exciting property of PDMPs, at least for statistical applications is that of {\em principled sub-sampling \citep{bierkens2019 , whereby randomly chosen subsets of data can be used to estimate likelihood values within the algorithm without biasing its invariant distribution.}}

elements.subsection-2411-016: Our contributions and summary of the results
elements.sent-2411-759: The goal of this work is to give the first rigorous theoretical results concerning the algorithm mixing deterioration caused by the excess switches induced by sub-sampling.
elements.sent-2411-760: To that end, we carry out a scaling limit analysis of Zig-Zag algorithm as the data set size $n \to \infty$, with dimension $d$ being fixed.
elements.sent-2411-761: Interplaying closely with the large-sample Bayesian asymptotics, we prove weak convergence results for the underlying PDMP for different sub-sampling versions of the Zig-Zag algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-762: The scaling limit approach has been a standard and impactful one in the literature to study mixing properties of MCMC algorithms with respect to dimension (\cite[e.g.][]{bierkens2022high,roberts1997weak,roberts2001 ) and dataset size \citep{schmon2021optimal, schmon2021large .}}
elements.sent-2411-763: Recently, there has been a growing interest in the non-asymptotic analysis of traditional MCMC methods (\cite{altmeyerPolynomialTimeGuarantees2022, chewi_optimal_2021, dwivedi_log-concave_2019 ), however, the non-asymptotic literature on PDMPs remains relatively limited (though see \cite{lu2022explicit, andrieu2021hypocoercivity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-764: In parametric Bayesian inference, under sufficient regularity conditions, it is well known that the posterior distribution converges to a degenerate distribution as $n \to \infty$ \citep{berk1970consistency, kleijn2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-765: The point of concentration, say $x_0$ if it exists and is unique, is the one minimizing the Kullback-Liebler (KL) divergence between the true data-generating distribution and the assumed model.
elements.sent-2411-766: We analyze the algorithm in two different phases: first, the {\it transient phase when the process is $O(1)$ distance away from $x_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-767: In this phase in the limit, any MCMC procedure is equivalent to an optimization routine seeking to find $x_0$ and so is Zig-Zag.
elements.sent-2411-768: In Section \ref{sec:transient , we show that as $n \to \infty$, the Zig-Zag trajectories behave like deterministic smooth flows with a drift pushing them in the direction of decreasing KL-divergence.}
elements.sent-2411-769: In particular, Theorem \ref{thm:transient establishes weak convergence of the Zig-Zag trajectories to the solutions of a system of ODEs.}
elements.sent-2411-770: These ODEs are explicitly characterized in equation \eqref{eq:limiting_drift .}
elements.sent-2411-771: We observe that while the canonical Zig-Zag attains optimal speed ($\pm 1$) in the limit, the sub-sampling versions remain sub-optimal with a damping factor that depends on the model.
elements.sent-2411-772: The fluid limit analysis in the transient phase captures large-scale movements (of $O(1)$) of the process.
elements.sent-2411-773: Under a sufficiently regular Bayesian model, for large $n$, the posterior concentrates within a $n^{-1/2}$-ball around $x_0$ and resembles a Gaussian \citep{kleijn2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-774: The high concentration of posterior mass restricts the movement of the Zig-Zag process.
elements.sent-2411-775: Consequently, close to $x_0$ the fluid limit analysis yields a trivial limit (see Theorem \ref{thm:canonical ).}
elements.sent-2411-776: Let $\hat{x _n$ be a sequence of suitable estimators of $x_0$ such that the Bernstein von-Mises Theorem holds: in the rescaled coordinate $\xi(x) = n^{1/2 (x - \hat{x _n)$, the posterior converges to a Gaussian distribution \citep{kleijn2012 .}}}}
